title: Session cookie is readable by JavaScript
summary: The application login sets the session cookie without the HttpOnly flag, so browser scripts can read it after login.
why_it_matters: Readable session cookies make XSS much more dangerous because injected JavaScript can steal active tokens directly from the browser.
manual_steps: Log in through the app at /login with a browser or interception proxy.
Inspect the Set-Cookie header on the login response.
Confirm that the session cookie is missing the HttpOnly attribute.
hint_ladder: Check the browser-facing app login flow instead of the API response.
Look for Set-Cookie on the redirect response after a successful login.
The flag is earned when the session cookie is missing HttpOnly and can be read by JavaScript.
what_to_learn: How cookie flags reduce the impact of client-side injection flaws.
Why HttpOnly matters even when tokens are signed.
How to describe token theft risk in a bug bounty report.
remediation: Set HttpOnly on the session cookie.
Use a stricter SameSite policy where possible.
Avoid storing long-lived bearer tokens in JavaScript-readable browser storage.
